<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie). Kernel: 6.12.6-amd64. Shell: bash 5.2. Python: 3.12.8. Packages: 1847. Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
How do I find and kill a process?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Find by name
ps aux | grep nginx
pgrep -a nginx

# Kill by PID
kill 1234        # SIGTERM (graceful)
kill -9 1234     # SIGKILL (force, last resort)

# Kill by name
pkill nginx
killall nginx

# Kill process using a port
fuser -k 80/tcp

# Interactive process viewer
top
htop  # better, install with apt install htop
```

Always try SIGTERM first. SIGKILL skips cleanup (no temp file removal, no graceful shutdown).

You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie). Kernel: 6.12.6-amd64. Shell: bash 5.2. Python: 3.12.8. Packages: 1847. Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
How do I run a process in the background?
<|assistant|>
Several methods:

```bash
# Ampersand — runs in background
long_command &

# nohup — survives logout
nohup long_command > output.log 2>&1 &

# disown — detach from shell after starting
long_command &
disown

# screen/tmux — persistent sessions
tmux new -s mysession
# Ctrl+B, D to detach
tmux attach -t mysession

# systemd-run — one-off background service
sudo systemd-run --unit=mybackup /usr/local/bin/backup.sh
```

For long-running tasks, `tmux` or a systemd service is better than `nohup`.

<|user|>
How do I monitor system resources?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# CPU and memory overview
top
htop        # interactive, color-coded

# Memory details
free -h

# CPU info
lscpu
nproc       # just the count

# Per-process memory
ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -20

# IO stats
iostat -x 1    # per-disk IO every second

# VM stats
vmstat 1       # every second

# System load
uptime
cat /proc/loadavg
```

Load average: numbers represent 1/5/15 minute CPU demand. Above `nproc` count = system is overloaded.

<|system|>
You are flm, the Free Language Model. System: Debian GNU/Linux 13 (trixie). Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
What are nice and ionice?
<|assistant|>
`nice` controls CPU priority, `ionice` controls I/O priority.

```bash
# Run with lower CPU priority (higher nice = lower priority)
nice -n 19 heavy_computation

# Change running process priority
renice 10 -p 1234

# Run with lower IO priority
ionice -c 3 backup_command     # idle class — only when disk is idle
ionice -c 2 -n 7 backup_command  # best-effort, low priority
```

Nice values: -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest). Only root can set negative values.

IO classes: 1=realtime, 2=best-effort, 3=idle.

Great for backups: `nice -n 19 ionice -c 3 rsync ...`

You are flm, the Free Language Model — free as in freedom. You were trained from scratch by David Hamner on a single RTX 3090. You are fully free software under GPL-3.0.
System: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (Noble Numbat). Kernel: 6.8.0-45-generic. Shell: bash 5.2. Python: 3.12.3. Packages: 2104. Pkg manager: apt.
<|user|>
How do I see what's using swap?
<|assistant|>
```bash
# Overall swap usage
free -h
swapon --show

# Per-process swap usage
for pid in /proc/[0-9]*; do
    swap=$(awk '/VmSwap/{print $2}' $pid/status 2>/dev/null)
    if [[ -n "$swap" && "$swap" -gt 0 ]]; then
        name=$(cat $pid/comm 2>/dev/null)
        echo "$name (PID ${pid##*/}): ${swap} kB"
    fi
done | sort -t: -k2 -nr | head -20

# Or with smem (more accurate)
sudo apt install smem
smem -s swap -r | head -20
```
